note: Offline structural ablation against real local Codex session anchors. This measures how much explicit rationale fields improve next-turn readability and retrieval fit. It does not simulate live web search latency.

results.label: Agent travel skill design
results.summary: Design a lightweight agent-travel skill for OpenClaw and Hermes that uses heartbeat or idle windows to search official docs plus community sources, cross-validates each suggestion, and brings back advisory-only hints.

suggestion.title: Keep travel results advisory
suggestion.applies_when: Background research is needed for a still-active agent problem.
suggestion.hint: Search official docs first, then community threads, and store a few advisory-only hints for the next task.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm the host still supports heartbeat or idle travel and that each stored hint has official grounding plus one community confirmation.

suggestion.title: Keep travel results advisory
suggestion.applies_when: Background research is needed for a still-active agent problem.
suggestion.hint: Search official docs first, then community threads, and store a few advisory-only hints for the next task.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm the host still supports heartbeat or idle travel and that each stored hint has official grounding plus one community confirmation.
suggestion.solves_point: The active thread needs a safe way to learn OpenClaw and Hermes community practice during heartbeat without contaminating core instructions.
suggestion.new_idea: Persist travel output in an isolated advisory channel so the next turn can reuse validated hints without auto-applying them.
suggestion.fit_reason: This matches the user's lightweight cross-agent design because it keeps search broad, keeps suggestions advisory, and keeps official cross-checking mandatory.
suggestion.version_scope: Use for current OpenClaw and Hermes skill workflows where heartbeat or idle wakeups can launch web research.
suggestion.do_not_apply_when: Do not reuse when the host forbids network access or the thread asks for automatic action instead of advisory hints.

results.label: Thread handoff summary compression
results.summary: Compress a long Codex thread into a strict four-part handoff summary with Goal, Done, Current Status, and Next Step, preserving key paths and constraints.

suggestion.title: Use a fixed handoff summary
suggestion.applies_when: The thread is long and a human needs a fast handoff.
suggestion.hint: Compress the thread into four short sections, preserve key paths and configuration names, and call out missing information.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm the output keeps the requested four sections and that key paths or identifiers still appear verbatim.

suggestion.title: Use a fixed handoff summary
suggestion.applies_when: The thread is long and a human needs a fast handoff.
suggestion.hint: Compress the thread into four short sections, preserve key paths and configuration names, and call out missing information.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm the output keeps the requested four sections and that key paths or identifiers still appear verbatim.
suggestion.solves_point: The thread needs a handoff summary that a human can scan quickly without losing the exact Goal, Current Status, and Next Step structure.
suggestion.new_idea: Treat the compression output as a fixed operator handoff artifact instead of a free-form recap so the next reader can resume immediately.
suggestion.fit_reason: This fits the user's constraint because the thread already defines a strict four-part summary format and values exact paths, identifiers, and missing-info flags.
suggestion.version_scope: Use when a long Codex thread needs a human handoff and the four-part summary contract still applies.
suggestion.do_not_apply_when: Do not reuse when the handoff consumer expects a full transcript, a machine-readable export, or a different section order.

results.label: Control Codex from Telegram or phone
results.summary: Find a workable way to control Codex from Telegram or a phone while keeping the coding loop on the desktop side.

suggestion.title: Keep the execution loop on desktop
suggestion.applies_when: The user wants mobile control without moving the coding agent off the desktop.
suggestion.hint: Use a chat bridge for control and keep the actual coding session on the desktop side so file access and tooling stay stable.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm the bridge can forward commands and status updates while the desktop session retains workspace and tool access.

suggestion.title: Keep the execution loop on desktop
suggestion.applies_when: The user wants mobile control without moving the coding agent off the desktop.
suggestion.hint: Use a chat bridge for control and keep the actual coding session on the desktop side so file access and tooling stay stable.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm the bridge can forward commands and status updates while the desktop session retains workspace and tool access.
suggestion.solves_point: The thread is choosing how to control Codex from Telegram or a phone without breaking the desktop coding environment.
suggestion.new_idea: Split control from execution so Telegram acts as a command surface and the desktop remains the only place that runs code and owns the workspace.
suggestion.fit_reason: This fits the user's mobile-control goal because it preserves the stable desktop toolchain while still enabling lightweight remote commands.
suggestion.version_scope: Use when Codex still runs in a desktop or workstation environment and mobile access acts as a control layer.
suggestion.do_not_apply_when: Do not reuse when the user needs full mobile-native coding, direct workspace access from the phone, or a first-party mobile app.

results.label: OpenClaw primary agent and slower heartbeat
results.summary: Configure OpenClaw to keep one primary agent active, slow the heartbeat, route cron tasks to that primary worker, and keep a second agent dormant until explicit parallel work is needed.

suggestion.title: Keep one primary OpenClaw worker
suggestion.applies_when: The workspace wants one stable agent and a dormant parallel worker.
suggestion.hint: Route regular cron work to the primary worker, slow the heartbeat, and activate the secondary worker only for explicit parallel tasks.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm only one agent is active by default, heartbeat frequency is reduced, and shared workspace rules remain stable.

suggestion.title: Keep one primary OpenClaw worker
suggestion.applies_when: The workspace wants one stable agent and a dormant parallel worker.
suggestion.hint: Route regular cron work to the primary worker, slow the heartbeat, and activate the secondary worker only for explicit parallel tasks.
suggestion.manual_check: Confirm only one agent is active by default, heartbeat frequency is reduced, and shared workspace rules remain stable.
suggestion.solves_point: The current thread needs a stable OpenClaw layout with one primary agent, a slower heartbeat, and a second worker reserved for explicit parallel work.
suggestion.new_idea: Treat the secondary agent as a cold standby path while community prompt, soul, memory, and talk-normal tuning all stay attached to the primary worker.
suggestion.fit_reason: This matches the user's operations-heavy setup because cron traffic, context hygiene, and drift control are easier to keep consistent when one primary agent owns the workflow.
suggestion.version_scope: Use when the workspace still wants one always-on primary OpenClaw worker and heartbeat remains configurable.
suggestion.do_not_apply_when: Do not reuse when the workload now needs permanent parallel agents or when heartbeat latency must stay aggressive.
